extracts.extract: It's not enough to define a topic you want to report on; you need to spell out exactly what you want to find out.
extracts.chapter: What Question or Questions Do You Want to Answer?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Defining precise research questions before reporting is a fundamental journalistic discipline unaffected by LLMs

extracts.extract: You'll want to evaluate how you can gather the most complete dataset or source pool possible. Would this be better accomplished through public records requests, existing datasets or gathering news clips? Or would those be useful in addition to crowdsourcing?
extracts.chapter: Can This Question Be Answered by Surveying the Public?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Evaluating data completeness across source types remains essential editorial judgment

extracts.extract: The foundation of a crowdsourced data project is your tip submission form, so you need to think very carefully about what data points you need, the phrasing of your questions and the terminology you use. The wording of your questions requires reporting just like anything else you publish.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Careful survey design and question wording are human judgment tasks that LLMs cannot replace

extracts.extract: Try to get it right the first time. Although as a technical matter you can change the form later, it adds lots of complexity to do so. If you're not careful, changing your form might require that you split your respondents into groups whose answers really can't be compared because the questions were worded differently.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Getting data collection right the first time to preserve comparability is a design principle independent of tooling

extracts.extract: As the lead newsroom, you should also be ready with resources like data dictionaries and documentation so reporters across the collaboration can understand the material. And you will likely understand the limitations of the data better than your partners will.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Providing data dictionaries and documentation for collaborative partners is an organizational need that persists

extracts.extract: Tips are just tips until they are verified by journalists, so it's a good idea to be cautious about how you cite numbers from the database.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Verifying crowdsourced tips before citing them as data is a core journalistic verification principle

extracts.extract: If a partner conducts its own analysis, it's a good idea to make sure it runs it by the other partners or with the group coordinator before publishing.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Peer review of analyses before publication is an enduring quality-control practice

extracts.extract: Collaboration requires that you are participating in good faith and that you trust the people who are going to be there. Participating in a collaboration is an act of trust and it's an act of courage, and you're doing that because of what you can accomplish together that you could not do by yourself.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Trust and good faith in collaborative journalism partnerships are human relational principles

extracts.extract: Project management is very important, and it's a completely undervalued position. Things don't happen by magic.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Project management as an undervalued but essential function in journalism is a timeless organizational insight
